title: Why the right hemisphere holds what the left hemisphere cannot name
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The left brain tells stories. The right brain holds the truth. 

 I spent years trying to talk my way out of trauma. I found the right therapist, the righ...
htmlContent:
The left brain tells stories. The right brain holds the truth.

I spent years trying to talk my way out of trauma. I found the right therapist, the right books, the right words. I could explain my childhood like a case study. I could name every defense mechanism, every attachment style, every trigger. I thought if I could just get the story right, my body would finally get the memo.

It didn't.

Here's the thing. The left hemisphere is a storyteller. It takes fragments of experience and weaves them into a coherent narrative. It's the part of your brain that says "I had a good childhood, mostly" or "My parents did their best" or "I'm fine now, I've processed it." The left brain craves order, sequence, meaning. It will smooth over contradictions to make a story that makes sense.

But the right hemisphere doesn't speak in stories. It speaks in sensations, images, body states, wordless knowing. It holds the stuff that never made it into language. The terror you felt at three years old that you never had words for. The shame that lives in your shoulders. The rage that sits in your jaw. The grief that lives in your chest like a stone.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades showing that trauma is not stored as a narrative. It's stored in the body, in the right hemisphere, in implicit memory. You can tell yourself a different story all day long. Your body will not be fooled.

Stay with me here. This is not about blaming your left brain for trying to help. It's about understanding that the left brain's job is to protect you from overwhelm. It will edit, reframe, minimize, intellectualize. It will say "I'm over it" when your nervous system is still bracing for impact. It will say "I forgive them" when your throat still tightens at the sound of their voice.

Your left brain is not lying. It's surviving.

The right brain speaks in a language your left brain forgot

When I first started working with a somatic therapist, she asked me to notice what was happening in my body when I talked about a particular memory. I said "I feel tight." She said "Where?" I said "My chest." She said "What shape is the tightness?" I thought she was insane.

But I tried. I closed my eyes. I noticed the tightness was round, dense, like a fist. It had a color. It had a temperature. It had a texture. And as I stayed with it, something shifted. The fist loosened. My eyes filled with tears I hadn't felt coming. A sound came out of me that wasn't a word.

That sound was the right hemisphere speaking. It had been waiting a long time.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, calls this "pendulation." The nervous system moves between states of activation and settling, activation and settling. The right hemisphere tracks this process without words. It knows when you're safe and when you're not. It knows when you're dissociating. It knows when you're pretending to be fine.

Wild, right? Your left brain can be fully convinced that you're calm. Your right brain can be screaming that you're in danger. And you will feel confused about why you're exhausted, why you're irritable, why you can't sleep. Because the left brain's story and the right brain's truth are not aligned.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). Gabor Mate shows how the suppression of authentic emotion, the refusal of the right brain's signals, literally makes people sick. The body says no when the mind refuses to.

Why words alone can't reach the right hemisphere

This is where a lot of talk therapy falls short. Not because talk therapy is bad. It's not. But because trauma lives in a part of the brain that doesn't process language the way the left brain does.

The right hemisphere is connected to the body more directly. It receives information from the viscera, the muscles, the skin. It processes emotion through the limbic system. It communicates through tone of voice, facial expression, eye contact, rhythm, touch. It doesn't want a logical explanation. It wants to feel safe.

Think about it. When a baby is distressed, you don't explain to them why they're upset. You hold them. You rock them. You make soothing sounds. You regulate your own nervous system so they can borrow your calm. That's right hemisphere to right hemisphere communication.

For those of us with complex trauma, that early communication often went wrong. Our caregivers were dysregulated, absent, threatening, or overwhelmed. Our right brains learned that the world was not safe. And no amount of adult logic has been able to override that learning. Because the learning is not stored in logic. It's stored in the body.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium glycinate is easily absorbed and supports the GABA receptors, which help the nervous system settle. It's not a cure. But for some people, it helps the body feel safe enough to let the right hemisphere speak.

How to start listening to the right hemisphere

You don't have to become a somatics expert overnight. You don't have to quit talk therapy or burn your journals. But you can start building a bridge between your left brain and your right brain. Here's what that looks like in practice.

First, slow down. The right hemisphere processes slower than the left. It needs time to register sensation, to feel, to integrate. If you're rushing through your day, your right brain is shouting into a hurricane. Try pausing for thirty seconds. Close your eyes. Take a breath. Notice what you notice.

Second, stop trying to name everything. When you feel something uncomfortable, your left brain wants to label it immediately. "I'm anxious." "I'm sad." "I'm triggered." Labels can be useful, but they can also shut down the process. Instead of naming, try describing. "There's a pressure in my chest. It feels hot. It's moving up into my throat." No story. No judgment. Just observation.

Third, let your body move. The right hemisphere is connected to motor function. Trauma often gets frozen in the body. Your nervous system may have been bracing for an attack that never came, and that bracing became chronic. Gentle movement, shaking, stretching, even just swaying can help discharge that stored energy. Peter Levine talks about "completed action." Your body needs to finish the response it couldn't finish when the trauma happened.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana's work shows how understanding your nervous system states can help you recognize when your right brain is in survival mode and how to invite it back to safety.

The right brain doesn't need you to fix it

This is the hardest part for me. I want to fix things. I want to understand, to solve, to move on. But the right hemisphere doesn't respond to fixing. It responds to presence.

When you sit with a sensation without trying to change it, something shifts. When you let a feeling be there without judging it, it starts to move. When you stop telling your body it's wrong to feel what it feels, your body starts to trust you.

I think of it like this. The left brain is the librarian. It catalogs, organizes, files things away. The right brain is the archive. It holds everything that couldn't be filed, everything that didn't fit the story, everything that was too much or too strange or too painful to be named.

You don't need to burn down the library. You just need to visit the archive. To sit with the unnamed things. To let them be seen, even if they can't be said.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link). Peter Levine's book walks through how the body holds trauma and how we can gently release it without having to relive the worst moments.

I don't have a neat ending for this. I don't think there is one. The right hemisphere will always hold things the left brain cannot name. That's not a problem to solve. It's a truth to live with. Some of our deepest knowing will never become words. Some of our deepest wounds will never become stories. They will just be held, witnessed, allowed to be.

And maybe that's enough. Maybe being with what cannot be named is the real healing. Not understanding. Not fixing. Just staying. Just being present. Just letting the right hemisphere finally say what it has been trying to say all along.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Don't name it. Just notice.
